---
title: "Saltmarsh Morning-Glory (Ipomoea sagittata)"
slug: "ipomoea-sagittata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474985782/medium.jpegg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Saltmarsh Morning-Glory"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Solanales"
tax_family: "Convolvulaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Solanales", "Convolvulaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474985782/medium.jpeg" alt="Saltmarsh Morning-Glory" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/vita-sativum">
vita-sativum</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264520901">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Solanales
-   **Family:** Convolvulaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=131555)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in coastal habitats such as salt marshes and mangrove edges.

## Natural history notes

A twining vine characterized by its arrow-shaped leaves and large, showy pink to lavender funnel-shaped flowers. In Florida, it is found in coastal habitats such as salt marshes and mangrove edges. It grows during the warmer months and often climbs over surrounding vegetation, with its flowers attracting pollinators such as bees and butterflies.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-ipomoea-sagittata/)
-   [Local profile](https://plant-directory.ifas.ufl.edu/plant-directory/hydrocotyle-species/)
